walkthrough.title: Gather the Character Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's get Coup set up — it's super quick. Grab the 15 character cards from the box. There are five different characters, and you have three copies of each: Duke, Assassin, Contessa, Captain, and Ambassador. Each card has the character's name, artwork, and a summary of their action and blocking abilities. Give them all a good shuffle together into one face-down deck. This is the Court Deck.

walkthrough.title: Deal Influence Cards
walkthrough.text: Now deal two cards face down to each player. These are your influence cards — they represent your political power in the court. Keep them face down in front of you. You can peek at your own cards at any time, but never show them to anyone else. These two cards are your lifeline. Lose both, and you're out of the game!

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Starting Coins
walkthrough.text: Give each player 2 coins from the treasury. The treasury is just the pile of all remaining coins in the center of the table. In Coup, coins are power. You need them to launch coups, fund assassinations, and generally stay dangerous. The more coins you stockpile, the more threatening you become.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Treasury and Summary Cards
walkthrough.text: Place all the remaining coins in the center of the table — that's the treasury. If your copy came with player aid cards or summary cards, hand one to each player. These cards list every character's action and what blocks what, which is incredibly helpful for your first few games. Pick someone to go first — youngest player, last person to tell a lie, whatever you want — and you're ready to start scheming!

summary.title: Gather the Character Cards
bullets: 15 character cards total: 3 each of Duke, Assassin, Contessa, Captain, Ambassador
Shuffle all 15 into one face-down Court Deck

summary.title: Deal Influence Cards
bullets: Deal 2 face-down cards to each player
These are your influence — peek at your own but keep them hidden
Lose both = eliminated from the game

summary.title: Hand Out Starting Coins
bullets: Each player starts with 2 coins
Coins come from the central treasury

summary.title: Set Up the Treasury and Summary Cards
bullets: Place remaining coins in center as the treasury
Hand out player aid cards if available
Pick a starting player — play goes clockwise

walkthrough.title: The Goal
walkthrough.text: The goal of Coup is simple: be the last player standing with at least one face-down influence card. Everyone else needs to get eliminated. You eliminate opponents by forcing them to lose influence — flip their cards face up. When both your cards are face up, you're done. Last person with a hidden card wins. It's cutthroat, it's fast, and it's glorious.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you take exactly one action. That's it — one action, then the next player goes. You don't draw cards, you don't roll dice, you just pick one thing to do. But here's where it gets spicy: some actions claim to use a specific character's power. You can claim ANY character action whether you actually hold that card or not. That's right — lying is not just allowed, it's the entire game.

walkthrough.title: General Actions — Anyone Can Do These
walkthrough.text: Three actions are available to everyone, no character required. Income: take 1 coin from the treasury. No one can block or challenge this. It's safe but slow. Foreign Aid: take 2 coins from the treasury. This is faster, but anyone claiming to have the Duke can block it. Coup: pay 7 coins to the treasury and force any player to lose one influence. They choose which card to flip. This cannot be blocked or challenged — it just happens. And here's the big rule: if you start your turn with 10 or more coins, you MUST coup. No excuses, no other action. You're too rich to sit still.

walkthrough.title: Character Actions
walkthrough.text: Each character has a special action. The Duke takes 3 coins from the treasury — called Tax. The Assassin pays 3 coins to assassinate a target, forcing them to lose one influence. The Captain steals 2 coins from another player. And the Ambassador lets you exchange cards with the Court Deck — draw 2 cards, choose which 2 to keep total, and return 2. The Contessa has no action of her own, but she's the only one who can block assassinations. Remember: you can claim ANY of these actions whether you hold the card or not!

walkthrough.title: Blocking
walkthrough.text: Some actions can be blocked by specific characters. The Duke blocks Foreign Aid — if someone takes Foreign Aid, anyone at the table can claim Duke and block it. The Contessa blocks Assassination — if someone tries to assassinate you, claim Contessa and the assassination fails (but you still keep your 3 coins if you're the assassin... wait, no — the assassin already paid. The target just doesn't lose influence). The Captain and Ambassador both block stealing — if someone tries to Captain-steal from you, claim Captain or Ambassador to block. And yes, you can bluff a block too. You can claim Contessa when you don't have her. Gutsy, but effective.

walkthrough.title: Challenges — Calling the Bluff
walkthrough.text: Here's the heart of Coup: any time someone claims a character — for an action OR a block — any other player can challenge them. When you challenge, you're saying 'I don't believe you have that card.' If the challenged player was BLUFFING (doesn't have the card), they lose one influence — flip a card face up. But if the challenged player was TELLING THE TRUTH, the challenger loses one influence instead. Plus, the honest player shuffles their revealed card back into the Court Deck and draws a new one. So challenging is risky! Get it right and you punish a liar. Get it wrong and you just punished yourself.

walkthrough.title: Assassination and Challenge Timing
walkthrough.text: Assassination has a special wrinkle. When someone declares an Assassin action against you, two things can happen before the kill resolves. First, anyone can challenge the Assassin claim. If the assassin was bluffing, they lose influence and the assassination fails — but the 3 coins are still spent. Second, if no one challenges (or the challenge fails), the target can claim Contessa to block. That block can itself be challenged! If the Contessa block is challenged and it was a bluff, the target loses an influence to the failed block challenge — and then ALSO loses one to the assassination. That's two influence gone, which usually means elimination. High stakes!

walkthrough.title: Losing Influence and Elimination
walkthrough.text: Whenever you lose influence, you choose one of your face-down cards and flip it face up. That card's character is now public information — everyone knows you had it. A face-up card can't be used, can't be claimed, can't be lost again. When both your cards are face up, you're eliminated and out of the game. Push your cards and coins aside and watch the remaining chaos unfold. The game continues until only one player has at least one face-down card. That player wins!

bullets: Be the last player with at least one face-down influence card
Eliminate opponents by forcing them to lose influence

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Court
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Coup! We're going to walk through a practice round with four players — Alex, Blake, Casey, and Dana. Each player has 2 face-down influence cards and 2 coins. Nobody knows what anyone else has. The tension is already building. Let's see how the scheming unfolds!

walkthrough.title: Alex Opens with Duke
walkthrough.text: Alex goes first and announces: 'I have the Duke. I'm taking Tax — 3 coins.' Alex takes 3 coins from the treasury, bringing their total to 5. Nobody challenges. Was Alex telling the truth? Maybe, maybe not. But challenging a Duke claim on the very first turn is risky — if Alex really has the Duke, the challenger loses influence for nothing. Smart players usually let early claims slide and gather information first.

walkthrough.title: Blake Takes Foreign Aid
walkthrough.text: Blake decides to take Foreign Aid — 2 coins, no character claim needed. But wait — anyone can claim Duke to block Foreign Aid! Alex already claimed Duke last turn. Do they block? Alex decides not to. Nobody else claims Duke either. Blake collects 2 coins, going from 2 to 4. Foreign Aid is decent income, but it's vulnerable to Duke blocks. If multiple players are claiming Duke, it becomes much harder to pull off.

walkthrough.title: Casey Claims Captain
walkthrough.text: Casey announces: 'I'm the Captain. I'm stealing 2 coins from Alex.' Alex now has a choice: let the steal happen, claim Captain or Ambassador to block, or challenge Casey's Captain claim. Alex decides to let it go. Casey takes 2 of Alex's coins. Alex drops from 5 to 3, and Casey jumps from 2 to 4. Stealing is aggressive but effective — it builds your coins while weakening a specific opponent.

walkthrough.title: Dana's Safe Play
walkthrough.text: Dana takes Income — just 1 coin from the treasury. No claim, no risk, no drama. Dana goes from 2 to 3 coins. Income is the safest action in the game because nobody can block or challenge it. But it's also the slowest. If you take Income every turn, you'll fall behind players who are claiming Duke for 3 coins or Captain-stealing 2. Use Income sparingly — when you need a quiet turn to observe.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake — Over-Challenging
walkthrough.text: Round two. Alex claims Duke again for Tax. Blake gets suspicious — Alex has claimed Duke twice now. 'I challenge!' Blake declares. Alex calmly flips over their card... it's the Duke. Alex was telling the truth! Blake loses one influence and flips a card face up. Alex shuffles the Duke back into the Court Deck and draws a new card. Ouch. Blake burned influence on a bad read. The lesson? Don't challenge just because someone has claimed a character multiple times. Consistency is actually a sign they might be telling the truth!

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Pick Your Challenges Carefully
walkthrough.text: Challenging is the highest-risk, highest-reward play in Coup. You want to challenge when you have real evidence — like you're holding the card someone claims, or you've seen two copies of it revealed. Random suspicion-based challenges will drain your influence fast. A good rule of thumb: don't challenge unless you'd bet your life on it. Because in Coup, you literally are.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake — Switching Claims
walkthrough.text: Blake is down to one influence and feeling desperate. On their turn, Blake claims Captain to steal from Casey. Next turn, Blake claims Ambassador to exchange cards. The turn after that, Blake claims Duke for Tax. Three different characters in three turns! Casey notices the pattern: 'There's no way Blake has all three of those. I challenge the Duke claim.' Casey is right — Blake was bluffing. Blake flips their last card face up and is eliminated. Switching characters every turn screams 'I'm bluffing!' and paints a massive target on your back.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Commit to Your Identity
walkthrough.text: Pick one or two characters to claim and stick with them. If you claim Duke every turn, people eventually stop challenging because consistency breeds credibility. If you swap characters constantly, everyone knows at least some of those claims are lies, and you'll get challenged at the worst possible moment. A believable lie repeated is safer than a new lie every turn.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake — Ignoring Coin Counts
walkthrough.text: Casey has been quietly stockpiling coins while Alex and Dana focus on each other. Casey hits 7 coins and announces: 'Coup on Dana.' Dana is stunned — they weren't tracking Casey's coins and didn't see this coming. Dana loses one influence. The coup was unblockable and unavoidable. If Dana had been watching Casey's coin pile, they could have stolen from Casey earlier or pressured Casey to spend coins on defense instead of saving for a coup.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Track the Coins
walkthrough.text: Coin counts are public information — use them! When someone hits 5-6 coins, they're one turn away from a coup. That's when you need to steal from them, assassinate them, or otherwise disrupt their plans. When someone hits 10 coins, they MUST coup — which means they're locked into targeting someone. The best players always know everyone's coin count and plan accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Alex Makes a Power Play
walkthrough.text: Back to the action. It's down to Alex, Casey, and Dana. Alex has 6 coins and announces: 'Assassin. I'm assassinating Casey. Paying 3 coins.' Casey can challenge the Assassin claim or try to block with Contessa. Casey decides to claim Contessa to block. Alex thinks about it... and challenges Casey's Contessa! Casey doesn't have the Contessa — it was a bluff. Casey loses one influence from the failed block. Then the assassination resolves and Casey loses another influence. Two cards flipped — Casey is eliminated! Bold plays win games.

walkthrough.title: The Final Showdown
walkthrough.text: It's Alex versus Dana, head to head. Alex has 3 coins and one influence card. Dana has 4 coins and one influence card. Every single action matters now. One wrong challenge, one successful assassination, and it's over. This is where all those small decisions — which claims you made, which challenges you did or didn't take — come together. Dana claims Duke for Tax to race toward coup money. Alex claims Captain to steal from Dana and slow them down. The mind games are intense when you both know the next mistake is the last one.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations!
walkthrough.text: You've just played through a full practice game of Coup! You've seen all the core actions, watched three common mistakes play out, and experienced the beautiful chaos of bluffing, challenging, and scheming. Remember: don't over-challenge on weak reads, commit to your claimed identity, and always keep one eye on everyone's coin count. You're ready to lie, steal, and coup your way to victory!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, let's cover the traps that catch new players. First, challenging too aggressively — every wrong challenge costs you influence, and influence is your health bar. Second, switching character claims every turn, which makes your bluffs transparent. Third, ignoring coin counts — if you're not tracking who's close to coup range, you'll get blindsided. Fourth, being too honest — if you only claim characters you actually have, experienced players will read you like a book. And fifth, hoarding coins past 10 without couping, which isn't even allowed! Keep these in mind and you'll outlast most beginners.

walkthrough.title: The Duke Opening
walkthrough.text: Claiming Duke on turn one is the most common opening in Coup, and for good reason. Tax gives you 3 coins instead of 1, tripling your income. Most players won't challenge a turn-one Duke claim because they have no information yet and the risk of losing influence is too high. Whether you actually have the Duke or not, this is one of the safest bluffs in the game. By turn two, you'll have 5 coins — almost enough to coup or assassinate.

walkthrough.title: When to Bluff vs. Play Honest
walkthrough.text: Bluffing is the soul of Coup, but you don't need to bluff every single turn. The best approach is a mix. Claim the characters you actually hold when possible — it's risk-free and builds credibility. Then sprinkle in bluffs when you need a specific ability, like blocking an assassination with a Contessa you don't have. The goal is to make your real claims and your fake claims look identical. If opponents can't tell the difference, they'll hesitate to challenge you on anything.

walkthrough.title: Reading Other Players
walkthrough.text: Coup is a people game. Watch how other players behave when they make claims. Do they hesitate before announcing a character? Do they look at their cards right before claiming? Do they suddenly change their claimed character after someone else reveals that card? These behavioral tells can help you decide when to challenge. Also track which cards have been revealed through eliminations — if two Dukes are face up on the table and someone claims Duke, you know they're lying.

walkthrough.title: Coin Management Strategy
walkthrough.text: Coins are the most important resource in Coup. At 7 coins, you can coup — which is unblockable and unchallengeable. That threat alone changes how people play around you. The sweet spot is hovering around 5-6 coins: threatening enough to coup next turn, but not yet forced to. At 10 coins, you MUST coup, which removes your options. Try to stay in the 5-7 range where you have maximum flexibility. If an opponent is climbing toward 7, steal from them or assassinate them before they get there.

walkthrough.title: Targeting and Table Politics
walkthrough.text: Who you target matters as much as what action you take. Stealing from the player with the most coins keeps the table balanced and makes you look reasonable. Assassinating the player with one influence left removes a competitor permanently. But be careful about piling onto one player — the rest of the table might turn on you next. In Coup, the biggest threat is often the quiet player nobody is targeting. Don't forget about them while you're feuding with someone else.

walkthrough.title: Blocking as a Power Move
walkthrough.text: Blocking is one of the most underrated tools in Coup. When someone tries to Foreign Aid, claiming Duke to block costs you nothing and denies them 2 coins. When someone tries to steal from you, blocking with Captain or Ambassador protects your pile. The beauty of blocks is that they're reactive — your opponent has already committed to their action, and now you get to decide whether to interfere. Even bluffing a block is relatively safe because the burden of proof shifts to the challenger.

walkthrough.title: The One-Influence Mindset
walkthrough.text: When you're down to one influence card, everything changes. You can't afford a single wrong challenge or failed bluff — one mistake and you're eliminated. Play conservatively: take Income instead of risky claims, avoid picking fights, and save your coins for one decisive coup. But also remember that other players know you're desperate, so a well-timed bold bluff can catch them off guard. Sometimes the best play when you're on the ropes is the most aggressive one, because nobody expects it.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't challenge too aggressively — wrong challenges cost influence
Don't switch character claims every turn — it screams bluff
Always track coin counts — surprise coups are preventable
Don't be 100% honest — experienced players will read you instantly

Sometimes the boldest play is the safest when you're expected to fold

walkthrough.title: The Credibility Bank
walkthrough.text: Think of your reputation as a bank account. Every truthful claim you make deposits credibility. Every successful bluff withdraws it — even if nobody caught you, the risk depletes your social capital. The best Coup players build up a huge credibility bank early by claiming characters they actually hold, then make one devastating bluff at the perfect moment. If you've truthfully claimed Duke five times and then bluff Duke once, nobody will challenge you. If you've been caught bluffing twice, even your honest claims will get challenged.

walkthrough.title: Character Card Counting
walkthrough.text: There are exactly 3 copies of each character in the deck. When cards get revealed through lost influence or successful challenges, that's permanent public information. Track it religiously. If two Contessas are face up, anyone claiming Contessa is either holding the last copy or bluffing — and the odds favor bluffing. If all three Dukes are accounted for, a Duke claim is a guaranteed bluff. This counting is the closest thing Coup has to a science, and it transforms your challenge decisions from gut feelings into calculated risks.

walkthrough.title: The Ambassador Power Play
walkthrough.text: The Ambassador is the most strategically powerful character in Coup, and most beginners underuse it. When you Ambassador-exchange, you draw 2 cards from the Court Deck, look at all your cards (up to 4 total), choose the best 2, and return the rest. This lets you ditch a useless character for a better one — or stack your hand with exactly the characters you've been claiming. It also lets you see cards in the deck, giving you information nobody else has. After an exchange, your claims become nearly unchallengeable because you might have literally just picked up that character.

walkthrough.title: Assassination Timing
walkthrough.text: Assassination is the most efficient elimination tool — 3 coins versus 7 for a coup. But timing is everything. The ideal assassination target is a player with one influence left, because it eliminates them outright. Assassinating a player with two influence is less efficient unless you're confident they can't block with Contessa. The worst time to assassinate is when you're down to one influence yourself — if your target challenges your Assassin claim and you were bluffing, you're eliminated instead. Save assassination for moments when you're confident and the target can't afford to challenge.

walkthrough.title: The Forced Coup Trap
walkthrough.text: Remember: at 10+ coins, you MUST coup. Smart opponents will manipulate you into this position. If you're sitting at 8-9 coins, you have one more turn of free choice before you're locked in. Some players deliberately let an opponent reach 10 coins because it forces them to spend 7 on a coup, dropping them back to 3 and removing their threatening position. The counter-play: don't hoard coins. Spend them at 7 via coup, or at 3 via assassination, to stay in the flexible 3-6 range where you have the most options.

walkthrough.title: Bluffing Contessa
walkthrough.text: Contessa is the most bluffed character in Coup because she's purely reactive — you only claim her when someone assassinates you, and the alternative is losing influence for free. This creates a fascinating dynamic. If someone assassinates you and you DON'T claim Contessa, you're basically admitting you don't have her (or that you're sacrificing a card strategically). So many players bluff Contessa that it's often correct to challenge Contessa blocks, especially against players who haven't been making Contessa-related claims earlier. But be careful — getting this wrong costs you influence, and the assassin's target keeps both their cards.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Player Table Dynamics
walkthrough.text: In games with 5-6 players, politics matters more than cards. The player who makes enemies early rarely survives to the endgame. Adopt a balanced approach: steal from the leader, avoid targeting the weakest player (who's not a threat), and don't block Foreign Aid unless there's a strategic reason. Let multiple players weaken each other while you build coins quietly. In larger games, the last three players standing are usually the ones who avoided drawing attention in the first half. Be dangerous enough that people don't target you freely, but subtle enough that you're nobody's first choice for a coup.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Theory — The Last Two
walkthrough.text: When it's down to two players, the game transforms completely. There's nobody else to target, no politics, just pure head-to-head deduction. Key principles: if you both have one influence, whoever coups first wins — so coin advantage is everything. If you have two influence and your opponent has one, assassination for 3 coins is cheaper than coup for 7. Track exactly which characters have been eliminated throughout the game — in a two-player endgame, you might know exactly what your opponent holds based on what's been revealed. This is where card counting pays off the most.

summary.title: The Credibility Bank
bullets: Truthful claims build credibility — bluffs withdraw it
Build trust early with honest plays, then bluff at the perfect moment
Players with high credibility rarely get challenged
Players caught bluffing get challenged even on honest claims

summary.title: Character Card Counting
bullets: 3 copies of each character — track every revealed card
2 of a character face up = third copy is rare, bluff more likely
All 3 accounted for = guaranteed bluff, free challenge
Card counting transforms challenges from guesses to calculations

summary.title: The Ambassador Power Play
bullets: Exchange lets you see up to 4 cards and pick the best 2
Swap out useless characters for ones matching your claims
Gain hidden information about what's in the Court Deck
Post-exchange claims are nearly unchallengeable

summary.title: Assassination Timing
bullets: 3 coins vs coup's 7 — much more efficient elimination
Best target: player with 1 influence (instant elimination)
Worst time: when you have 1 influence and might get challenged
Assassinate when confident target can't successfully block or challenge

summary.title: The Forced Coup Trap
bullets: 10+ coins = must coup, losing all other options
Smart opponents let you reach 10 to force a predictable move
Counter: spend at 7 (coup) or 3 (assassinate), stay in 3-6 range
Flexibility is more valuable than a massive coin pile

summary.title: Bluffing Contessa
bullets: Most bluffed character — only claimed when facing assassination
Not claiming Contessa when assassinated is a strong tell
Challenging Contessa blocks is often correct, especially vs. inconsistent players
Wrong challenge = you lose influence AND target keeps both cards

summary.title: Multi-Player Table Dynamics
bullets: In 5-6 player games, politics matters more than cards
Avoid making enemies early — aggressive players get eliminated first
Let others weaken each other while you build quietly
Be threatening enough to deter, subtle enough to avoid targeting

Coin advantage is decisive — whoever coups first often wins

Card counting is most powerful here — you may know exactly what they hold

entries.definition: One of five character roles. Action: Tax — take 3 coins from the treasury. Block: can block Foreign Aid. Three copies in the deck. The most commonly claimed character due to strong, safe income generation.

One of five character roles. Action: Assassinate — pay 3 coins to force a target player to lose one influence. Can be blocked by Contessa. Three copies in the deck. The most efficient elimination tool at 3 coins versus coup's 7.

One of five character roles. No action of her own. Block: blocks assassination — if targeted by an assassin, claiming Contessa prevents the influence loss. Three copies in the deck. The most commonly bluffed character since she's only claimed reactively.

One of five character roles. Action: Steal — take 2 coins from another player. Block: can block another player's steal attempt. Three copies in the deck. Strong for both offense (stealing) and defense (blocking steals).

One of five character roles. Action: Exchange — draw 2 cards from the Court Deck, look at all your cards, choose 2 to keep, and return the rest face-down. Block: can block another player's steal attempt. Three copies in the deck. Provides information advantage and hand optimization.
General action available to all players. Take 1 coin from the treasury. Cannot be blocked or challenged. The safest but slowest way to gain coins.
General action available to all players. Take 2 coins from the treasury. Can be blocked by anyone claiming Duke. Cannot be challenged since no character claim is made.

General action available to all players. Pay 7 coins to the treasury to force any player to lose one influence (they choose which card to flip). Cannot be blocked or challenged. Mandatory if you have 10 or more coins at the start of your turn.
Duke's character action. Take 3 coins from the treasury. Can be challenged by any player who doubts the Duke claim. The fastest income action in the game.

Captain's character action. Take 2 coins from a target player. If the target has only 1 coin, take that 1 coin. If the target has 0 coins, stealing is not allowed. Can be blocked by Captain or Ambassador. Can be challenged.

Assassin's character action. Pay 3 coins to force a target player to lose one influence. The 3 coins are paid to the treasury whether the assassination succeeds or not. Can be challenged (Assassin claim) or blocked (Contessa). If blocked, coins are still spent.

Ambassador's character action. Draw 2 cards from the Court Deck, look at all your cards (up to 4), choose 2 to keep, and return the rest face-down to the Court Deck. Can be challenged. Allows hand optimization and information gathering.

A reactive claim made to prevent another player's action from resolving. Duke blocks Foreign Aid. Contessa blocks Assassination. Captain and Ambassador block Stealing. Blocks can themselves be challenged. You can bluff a block just like you can bluff an action.

Any player can challenge any character claim — whether it's an action or a block. If the claim was a bluff, the bluffer loses one influence. If the claim was truthful, the challenger loses one influence and the honest player reshuffles their revealed card into the Court Deck and draws a new one.

Your face-down character cards represent your influence in the court. Each player starts with 2 influence. Losing influence means flipping a card face up permanently. When both cards are face up, you are eliminated. Influence is essentially your health — every point lost is irreversible.

The currency of Coup. Players start with 2 coins. Coins are gained through Income (1), Foreign Aid (2), Tax (3), or Stealing (2). Coins are spent on Assassination (3) and Coup (7). Coin counts are always public information. At 10+ coins, you must coup.

The face-down pile of character cards in the center of the table. Contains 15 cards total (3 each of 5 characters) minus those dealt to players. Used for Ambassador exchanges and for reshuffling cards after successful challenges. Cards returned to the deck should be shuffled in.
